# SERIES-FACTS.md — The Cathedral Close Mysteries
## Master Canon Document — DO NOT EDIT WITHOUT SERIES-MANAGER REVIEW
## Last updated: 2026-05-03 | Source: Book 1 — Death in the Cathedral Close

---

## DOCUMENT RULES
- This is the single source of truth for all Cathedral Close Mysteries books
- Individual book FACTS.md files feed INTO this document, not the other way around
- Any writer agent must read this document in full before starting any new book
- Any fact here that conflicts with a new manuscript = the manuscript is wrong
- SPOILER SECTIONS are clearly marked — series-continuity-guardian has access; other agents do not need to read them
- CONTINUITY CONFLICTS flagged in the Book 1 CONTINUITY-REPORT.md are noted here with their recommended resolution so future books use the corrected facts

---

## SECTION 1 — LOCKED CHARACTERS

### DR HELEN MARSH (Protagonist)

**Age at Book 1:** 70
**Profession:** Retired forensic pathologist. 35 years in the coroner's office. Now a volunteer in the Wychford Cathedral archive.
**Home:** No. 7, the close
**Marital status:** Widowed. Husband David died 8 years before Book 1. A grandfather clock that was David's stands in Helen's front room.
**Children:** One son — Julian Marsh, 35, estranged for 2 years before Book 1 (his choice). Partial reconciliation by end of Book 1.
**Physical description:** NOT ESTABLISHED IN BOOK 1 beyond the following details. Establish carefully in Book 2.
- Always wears a single strand of pearls — a 35-year habit from court testimony. LOCKED.
- Wears a navy wool dressing gown, owned since David was alive.
- Wears a tweed coat and pearls when attending crime scenes (her court habit).
- Carries a small spiral-bound notebook, kept from her court days.
- Physical build, height, hair colour, eye colour: NOT ESTABLISHED IN BOOK 1.

**Internal architecture:**
- Want (external): to find out what really happened to Marcus
- Need (internal): to reconnect with the part of herself that still matters — she retired feeling erased
- Fear: that Julian's contempt for her is deserved
- Flaw: emotional distance masquerading as professional detachment
- Ghost: her marriage ended because she was always at work; Julian blames her
- Arc (Book 1): begins useful but isolated; ends necessary and beginning to let someone in

**Speech and thought patterns (see also Section 7):**
- Short declarative sentences. Never volunteers emotion. Understatement.
- Never says "I think" — she says "I note."
- Characteristic line: "He didn't fall, Edmund. Look at his hands."
- Helen's professional formulation (locked): "motive before method is the correct order. Method without motive is mechanics."
- Her internal verdict on Rosalind: "She is a good person who did a terrible thing. That is not a contradiction. That is the hardest kind of case." LOCKED.
- On being right: "Being right is not the same as being finished." LOCKED.
- On why she stayed in pathology after David died: "Because stopping felt like agreeing with something I wasn't ready to agree with." LOCKED.
- On Julian: "That may also be true." (on whether she was doing the work instead of David) LOCKED.

**Habits and domestic details:**
- Kitchen wall calendar: the cathedral printed annual schedule, posted to every close residence in January.
- Notebook entry discipline: she wrote only the word "mug" in her notebook on the morning of the body. Relevant: she writes one word per observation, not sentences.
- Tea: makes it, does not always drink it. (Made tea and did not drink it after Rosalind's visit, Ch 27.)
- Archive: Helen has her own key. She works Wednesday volunteer sessions on 15th-century bishop's correspondence at Book 1's end.
- She poured cold coffee down the cellar sink before leaving for the arrest. (Domestic precision as emotional control.)
- Helen's professional response to a cold crime scene: Helen examines first, speaks second.

**Relationships at end of Book 1:**
- Edmund Hale: closest partner in the investigation; trust established. She can hear Chapter meetings from the archive.
- Julian Marsh: estrangement door no longer locked. "Yes. All right." to Julian's offer to pick up if she rings.
- DS Claire Marsh: complex, respectful. Helen names Rosalind to Claire first (Ch 19). Claire trusts Helen's instincts.
- Rosalind Ferris: grief for what Rosalind was and did. Helen watched the arrest from her window at No. 7. Did not cross to No. 4 during or after.

---

### CANON EDMUND HALE (Co-protagonist)

**Age at Book 1:** 68
**Role:** Cathedral lawyer — ecclesiastical law, Chapter disputes, property matters. Takes minutes at Chapter meetings as cathedral legal officer.
**Home:** No. 2, the close
**Background:** Has been at Wychford 22 years (arrived at age 46). Never married. Has a quiet, genuine faith.
**Physical description:** NOT ESTABLISHED IN BOOK 1 beyond the following. Establish carefully in Book 2.
- Carries a battered brown leather notebook everywhere. Never uses his phone for notes. LOCKED.
- Uses a green ballpoint pen exclusively for audit work.
- No physical appearance details (height, build, hair) established in Book 1.

**Internal architecture:**
- Want (external): to protect the cathedral from the damage this murder will do
- Need (internal): to accept that protecting an institution sometimes means exposing its failures
- Fear: that faith without action is cowardice dressed as patience
- Flaw: institutional loyalty — protects the cathedral even when it shouldn't be protected
- Ghost: covered up a minor scandal fifteen years ago that he still regrets
- Arc (Book 1): begins loyal defender of the close; ends willing to expose its failures if the truth requires it

**Speech and thought patterns (see also Section 7):**
- Longer sentences that double back on themselves.
- Uses "I wonder if..." when he means "I know." His notebook orthography: underlining "wonder" twice = "I already know."
- Occasional Latin — always translated immediately, always wryly. Example: *Stultissimus* — used privately; no English translation offered in the text.
- Characteristic lines:
  - "She was telling the truth — not about the murder, but about herself." LOCKED (on Rosalind).
  - "He was, in his way, a better person than the circumstance allowed him to be." LOCKED (on Marcus).
  - "She had thirty years of say in it. The momentum doesn't stop the week she leaves." LOCKED.
  - "I chose truth. The institution survived it. I had not expected that." (notebook entry, May 1) LOCKED.
  - On looking away from evidence: "He had confused looking away with discretion. He will not make this confusion again." LOCKED.
  - "Edmund: "She was very good at this." (watching Rosalind chair the Chapter meeting) LOCKED.

**Notebooks (CRITICAL continuity detail):**
- Investigation notebook: battered brown leather, used throughout Book 1. Approximately 30 pages pre-investigation + 40 pages investigation entries. Now full. Placed in Edmund's bookcase under the glazed door at end of Book 1.
- New notebook: amber cover, purchased from the cathedral precinct shop. His seventeenth notebook from this shop. Pages 1–3 used by end of Book 1. LOCKED.
- The notebook's double underline coding: "wonder" underlined twice = certainty disguised as speculation. This is his private shorthand; no other character knows it.

**Edmund's emotional state regarding growing feelings for Helen:**
- The BLUEPRINT establishes that Edmund has growing (unspoken) feelings for Helen. These are NOT declared in Book 1. They are NOT named in the text. They are present as subtext only.
- SERIES-PLAN: feelings declared in Book 2, accepted in Book 3. Do not advance this ahead of schedule.

**Office and professional details:**
- Edmund's office: Chapter House annex, first floor. Wood-panelled. View over the cloister garth.
- Cherrywood table by the window, used for laying out documents.
- Rosewood bookcase with glazed door (green lever-arch case file now inside this bookcase).
- Office door has an 1893 iron lock; key kept in keyhole on the inside.
- Chapter House office window: gap at bottom of frame, never fully sealed in forty years; rain smell comes through.
- Edmund's pen: green ballpoint for audit work.

**Relationships at end of Book 1:**
- Helen Marsh: trusted partner. Withheld the £83 concordance from her from Wednesday to Monday (Ch 22–28). She noted this. He did not apologise. The partnership survived this.
- DS Claire Marsh: functional working relationship through the investigation; respect.
- Rosalind Ferris: grief. "Not betrayal. Comprehension without resolution." He will provide a character statement at sentencing.
- Webb: felt "something close to shame" watching Webb leave the police station (Ch 28). LOCKED.
- Margaret Holt: she is devoted to him. Institutional warmth.

---

### DS CLAIRE MARSH (Supporting — recurring)

**Age at Book 1:** 42
**Role:** Detective Sergeant, Wychford CID. Helen's former junior at the coroner's office — Helen mentored her when Claire was a trainee pathologist before she switched to police.
**Physical description:** Short, dark hair. No-nonsense manner.
**Speech pattern:** Police-precise. Does not use people's first names on duty. Shifts to first names with Helen only when the police frame falls away. DS Claire's words: "Oh God" (at sight of Helen on scene). "Give me an hour." "Show me everything. From the beginning."
**Status at end of Book 1:** Active. Rosalind charged. Series-plan notes she is promoted to DI between Books 1 and 2 — do not contradict this in Book 2.
**Key relationship:** Respects Helen enormously; complex loyalty. She wrote down Edmund's observation about Marcus without comment (Ch 38).

---

### JULIAN MARSH (Supporting — recurring)

**Age at Book 1:** 35
**Relationship to Helen:** Her son. Estranged for 2 years before Book 1 (his choice). Lives and works in Bristol (data analytics).
**Physical description:**
- Has David Marsh's (his father's) eyes.
- Has his grandmother's stubbornness.
- Scar on the back of his right hand — bicycle accident, age 11. Helen cried in A&E; Julian managed the adults.
**Speech pattern:** Brisk, defensive. Long pauses before honest statements. "I'm fine" means he is not fine.
**Key facts from Book 1:**
- Received a £12,000 loan from Marcus nine years ago, during the period of his estrangement from Helen. Never repaid. Marcus demanded £17,000 (principal + interest) approximately six weeks before the murder.
- Travelled to Wychford on the 7am Bristol service to negotiate in person. Argued with Marcus at the close gate the afternoon before the death — about the debt.
- Has Helen's spare close gate key (still in his possession at end of Book 1).
- His confirmed alibi (corrected per CONTINUITY-REPORT.md Conflict C1): King's Head pub, approximately 7pm, departed around 9:30pm, two pints.
- At approximately 9:30pm he saw Marcus leaving Rosalind's house at No. 4, walking steadily toward the north transept. This is the pivotal witness testimony. Marcus did not see Julian.
- Stayed at King's Head for at least 3 nights after the murder.
- Helen invited Julian to dinner at No. 7 — first shared meal in 2+ years.
**Status at end of Book 1:** Estrangement door no longer locked. Returned first weekend of May, ostensibly to collect a jacket from King's Head (pretext acknowledged by both). Offered to pick up if Helen rings; she accepted. Lives in Bristol.
**Open thread for Book 2:** The relationship is unlocked, not repaired. Julian may appear again — the pretext of a visit would be transparent and acknowledged.

---

### THOMAS OAKES (Supporting — recurring)

**Age at Book 1:** 58
**Role:** Night verger. Has been the night verger for 43 years. Closes the cathedral each evening, does the 11pm rounds of the close.
**Location:** Small office behind the south porch.
**Key facts:**
- Found Marcus's body at approximately 6:50am during Morning Prayer setup.
- Closed the open stair door before realising what he was looking at.
- Saw a figure in a pale blue cardigan leaving the organ loft stair at 4 past 11pm on the night of the murder — from 20 paces, from behind, for approximately 4 seconds. Assumed it was Rosalind. This became pivotal evidence.
- His niece rang him at approximately 9:45pm the night of the murder (family matter); he was 20–25 minutes late on rounds as a result.
- Carries the bell-ringing schedule on Thursdays — a single folded sheet.
- Attends Morning Prayer — stands against the south transept pillar (his usual position). Present at post-arrest Morning Prayer (Ch 36), hands on knees, not comfortable and not performing comfort.
**Status at end of Book 1:** Active. Continues as night verger.

---

### MARGARET HOLT (Supporting — recurring)

**Age at Book 1:** 75
**Role:** Chapter clerk. Has worked for the cathedral for 40 years. Institutional memory of the close. Devoted to Edmund.
**Speech pattern:** Institutional memory deployed as comedy. "Oh, we had something similar in 1987. Canon Fforbes. Very nasty. Though he did survive."
**Habits:** Brings homemade shortbread to Chapter meetings. Converted the small chair room beside the vestry into an informal tea room circa 1987.
**Status at end of Book 1:** Active. Unofficial co-ordinator of choir schedule post-arrest, alongside Father Reeve. Present at post-arrest Morning Prayer (third row north).

---

### FATHER SIMON REEVE (Supporting — recurring)

**Age at Book 1:** 40
**Role:** Cathedral chaplain — younger, less politically experienced.
**Relationship to Marcus:** Liked him personally. Heard Marcus through the Chapter House wall two Wednesdays before the murder — Marcus's words: "It is not your decision" and "I have told you what I am going to do and I am going to do it." (Context was ambiguous; applied to Rosalind on re-read.)
**Status at end of Book 1:** Leading choir rehearsals and Morning Prayer responses in Rosalind's place. Lost his naive faith in the close during the investigation; kept the real faith. Described in Ch 38 as imperfect and real.

---

### ROSE HARTLEY (Supporting — series thread character)

**Age at Book 1:** 38
**Role:** Song school administrator. Descended from the Hartley family (the 1887 east transept matter).
**Physical description:** Left-foot asymmetry gait characteristic (~0.18–0.22 asymmetry index from old ankle injury). This gait was used to eliminate her as the CCTV figure.
**Song School key:** Holds RKF-003.
**Series thread status:** Rose does not know the full significance of her family's connection to the cathedral. She was a background character throughout Book 1. Central to Book 2.
**What she must NOT know in Book 1 / early Book 2:** Robert Hartley's name, Canon Aldous Pemberton's name, the specific nature of the 1887 cover-up.
**What she does know (implied):** She is a Hartley. She works in the song school. She is aware of some family history with the cathedral — the degree of this awareness is NOT ESTABLISHED IN BOOK 1. Establish carefully in Book 2.
**Series-plan note for Book 2:** Rose will become a central figure. She knows more about her family's history than she initially admits. She will eventually reveal: "There's one more thing. My grandmother left a box. I haven't opened it." (Book 2 closing hook.)

---

### CANON ALISTAIR WEBB (Supporting — recurring)

**Age at Book 1:** 55
**Role:** Canon with pastoral responsibilities.
**Key facts:**
- Lost the Director of Music position to Marcus 15 years ago — has resented Marcus since.
- Married to Anne Webb.
- Was having an affair with Sarah Clifton, married choir soprano (Vicars' Row; husband works in Manchester during the week). Affair began approximately 3 years before Book 1. Marcus knew about the affair for 3 years and said nothing.
- His secret throughout the investigation was the affair, not murder. His alibi: with Sarah Clifton, Vicars' Row, 9pm–approximately 10:30–10:45pm, night of the murder.
- Has never held a Song School key — confirmed by 30 years of ledger records and the 1998 Chapter resolution.
- Falsely arrested in Chapter 26. Released 4:30pm Monday after CCTV enhancement confirmed Rosalind's cardigan.
- His exit from police station: nodded once to Edmund, did not speak, walked out into Minster Lane without looking back. LOCKED.
**Status at end of Book 1:** Cleared. Affair now known to Edmund (and presumably Anne). Present at post-arrest Morning Prayer (far side, hands on knees).

---

### VERY REVD PATRICIA KNOWLES (Supporting — recurring)

**Age at Book 1:** 64
**Role:** The Dean — chief administrator of Wychford Cathedral. Has been Dean for 8 years. First female Dean of Wychford — still a political fact in the Chapter.
**Key facts from Book 1:**
- Was in secret negotiations to sell cathedral property (12 acres glebe land, Wychford Abbots road) to a developer to fund urgent structural repairs (south tower cracking since 1987). Against Chapter rules, but motivated by genuine necessity. No contract signed; not a breach of the Cathedrals Measure.
- Her first question after Marcus's death: "Will this affect Easter services?" Read as cold; was red herring.
- Called a special emergency Chapter meeting (not the usual first Tuesday) during the investigation.
- In "consultations" with the bishop post-arrest — understood as damage management.
**Status at end of Book 1:** Active as Dean. Series-plan: central to Book 3 as the one person in the close who eventually knows the full truth about 1887. She is found dead in Book 3.

---

### SARAH CLIFTON (Minor — mentioned only)

**Role:** Married choir soprano. Lives in Vicars' Row. Husband works in Manchester during the week.
**Key fact:** Webb's affair partner, 3+ years. Confirmed Webb's alibi to Edmund in her sitting room at Vicars' Row (gas fire recently extinguished, husband away). Her statement relayed to Claire.

---

### ANNE WEBB (Minor — mentioned only)

**Role:** Alistair Webb's wife.
**Key observed fact:** At Memorial service she held Webb's hand unusually. At Webb's arrest she reached for his arm, stopped herself — hand held in mid-air between them. This detail is emotional texture, not plot, but is locked as a character moment.

---

### MARCUS VAYNE (Victim — deceased)

**Age at death:** 58
**Role:** Cathedral organist and director of music, 25 years at Wychford.
**Character (revealed posthumously):**
- Brilliant musician. Difficult colleague — three formal complaints in his personnel file, all resolved without action.
- Had a heart condition: took digoxin (0.125mg daily), beta-blockers, and statins. Medication kept in his desk drawer at the Song School.
- Kept a locked box in his organ bench containing love letters from Catherine Vane (deceased 19 years ago, cancer, swift). The box also contained: a pressed flower (species unidentifiable), a Bristol Philharmonia concert ticket stub (February, 19 years ago), a black-and-white photograph of Catherine at Tintern, 1999. Marcus attended her funeral.
- Edmund's notebook entry: "He loved someone named Catherine for thirty years without anyone knowing." LOCKED.
- Marcus had loaned Julian £12,000 nine years ago without telling Helen.
- Marcus discovered Rosalind's embezzlement approximately 3 months before his death. He gave her the opportunity to repay quietly, then withdrew that offer and insisted on disclosure to the Chapter. He was being patient with her during the period she was simultaneously planning to kill him.
- Marcus filed a formal complaint letter to the Bishop about financial irregularities (dated 14 March) — signed for in the correspondence ledger by Rosalind, who never forwarded it. The forwarding column remains blank.
- Marcus knew about Webb's affair with Sarah Clifton for three years and said nothing.
- Marcus had a sister in Cumbria. Edmund wrote to her about the personal items.
- Marcus composed at least two pieces of his own music.
- Used a 1962 Olivetti typewriter; refused email.
- Had a mid-letter in the typewriter the night of his death: about a tenor passage in the Salve Regina.
- His pocket diary recorded only "practice" for the night of his death.
- Edmund's locked formulation: "He was, in his way, a better person than the circumstance allowed him to be." LOCKED.
- At the moment of his death: Marcus thanked Rosalind for the coffee. He was grateful. It was cold. LOCKED.

**Physical description at death:**
- Body was supine at the foot of the north transept stair, head turned a quarter to the left, arms at sides, legs straight, right ankle slightly crossed over left. Position wrong for a fall — hands undamaged.
- Wearing his green cardigan when found.
- Glasses 2 feet from him at the base of a pillar; right lens cracked across.
- A music score lay fanned open approximately 2 yards behind him on the floor.

---

### CANON ROSALIND FERRIS (Killer — convicted)
### SEE ALSO SECTION 4 (KILLER ARCHIVE) FOR FULL SPOILER DETAIL

**Age at Book 1:** 62
**Role:** Precentor — responsible for all cathedral music and worship.
**Home:** No. 4, the close. Oxford blue front door. Bay tree on step. Boot scraper worn smooth. Narcissi in terracotta pot on front windowsill. Piano visible through the front window.
**Tenure at Wychford:**
- CONTINUITY NOTE: FACTS.md character table states "30 years." Chapter 5 states "came year after Marcus" (which would be ~24 years if Marcus is 25 years). CONTINUITY-REPORT.md recommends Option B as the fix: remove "came year after Marcus" and treat Rosalind as having arrived six years BEFORE Marcus (i.e., she came first). This makes her 30-year tenure and his 25-year tenure coherent and adds character depth (she has deeper roots). Book 2 should use 30 years for Rosalind, 25 years for Marcus, with Rosalind having preceded him.
**Physical details:**
- Pale blue cashmere cardigan — powder pale blue (not pale grey-blue), soft, hip-length, buttons, small folded collar, no hood. Worn constantly; becomes the key evidence. It is in police custody at end of Book 1.
- Silver treble clef brooch, worn at Chapter meetings.
- Height: approximately 5'6"–5'7" (consistent with CCTV figure).
- Stride asymmetry index: approximately 0.04 (near-symmetrical — she walks evenly under pressure).
- Hair: kept up or in a scarf (consistent with Thomas's account and CCTV figure).
**Status at end of Book 1:** Arrested 11:41am, Thursday, approximately Day 50 of the investigation. Entered a guilty plea. Charge: murder. Embezzlement concurrent in separate proceedings. Sentencing: autumn of the year of the story. Edmund will provide a character statement.
**What she said at arrest:** She opened the door and said "Come in, then." She was already dressed. LOCKED.
**What she said from custody:** Asked DS Claire whether the services were continuing. Told yes. Said: "Good. That's the right thing." LOCKED.

---

### BRIDGET FERRIS (Deceased — key backstory figure)

**Relationship:** Rosalind's mother.
**Status:** Died 18 months before Book 1, aged 87.
**Death notice:** Bristol Evening Post, three lines only. "Bridget Mary Ferris, aged 87, peacefully. Much loved mother. Private funeral arrangements." No mention of Wychford. No mention of the cathedral.
**Care:** Clifton Convalescent Home, Clifton, Bristol. Specialist private dementia care, established 1989. Fees approximately £4,000/month. Rosalind's salary covered part of this; she supplemented approximately £7,000/year from the cathedral music fund.
**Identity used in crime:** Rosalind used her mother's identity documents from the estate to obtain the fraudulent second digoxin prescription. The dispensing record names "Mrs. B. Ferris, Clifton Convalescent Home" — though Bridget was already dead at the time of collection (dispensed 11 March; Bridget died 18 months earlier).

---

## SECTION 2 — LOCKED WORLD

### WYCHFORD CATHEDRAL CLOSE — Geography

**Nature:** Medieval walled enclosure. Self-contained community with its own rhythms.
**Residential properties:** 12 properties, numbered 1–12. Approximately 40 permanent residents.
**Access:** The close gate is locked at 11pm. Residents have gate keys. Visitors must be buzzed in. (Julian used Helen's spare gate key on the night of the murder — a key he still holds at Book 1's end.)
**Key properties:**
- No. 2 — Edmund Hale's house
- No. 3 — Canon Alistair Webb's house (where he was arrested 7:40am Monday)
- No. 4 — Canon Rosalind Ferris's house (vacant from Book 2 onward). Oxford blue door. Bay tree on step. Boot scraper worn smooth. Front window has room with piano visible.
- No. 7 — Dr Helen Marsh's house
- Vicars' Row — Sarah Clifton's residence (nearby, not numbered in the close proper)
- Deanery — the Dean's residence (not numbered in the close; described as separate)
- Thomas Oakes's office: small office behind the south porch

**Lych-gate bench:** Used by Helen and Julian for a conversation (Ch 7). The bench by the lych-gate exists as a named location.

**The 1880 close map:** Found uncatalogued by Helen in the archive (Ch 39). The song school is shown in a slightly different position (slightly south, smaller) than the present building. The Precentor's house has always been No. 4.

---

### THE CATHEDRAL ITSELF

**Age and nature:** Medieval English cathedral. Seven centuries of building. Present-day functioning cathedral — daily services, choir, organ. Wychford Cathedral.

**The Organ Loft:**
- 18 feet above the nave floor.
- Accessible only via a narrow stone stair from the north transept.
- One door at the base of the stair.
- The stair door scrapes audibly on the threshold; the sound is recognisable.
- No other access. The gallery is 18 feet above the nave floor — a fatal fall.
- The organ can be heard from Helen's sitting room window during cathedral rehearsals.
- The organ can be heard from the archive (Helen confirmed "I could hear the meeting from the archive" in the context of Ch 40 — she could hear Chapter meetings, implying general acoustic penetration of the close).

**The Archive:**
- Cellar beneath the Chapter House. Helen has her own key.
- Cold, damp. Contains parish records back to 1347.
- Has a cellar sink.
- Current working files at Book 1's end: fifteenth-century bishop's correspondence (resumed after the investigation).
- 1880s financial records box is set aside by Helen's note: "Please do not re-file. To be looked at." Initialled by Helen.

**Edmund's Office:**
- Chapter House annex, first floor.
- Wood-panelled. View over the cloister garth.
- Cherrywood table by the window for laying out documents.
- Rosewood bookcase with glazed door (now holds the green lever-arch case file from Book 1).
- 1893 iron lock on the door; key kept in keyhole on the inside.
- Chapter House office window: gap at bottom of frame, never fully sealed in 40 years; rain smell comes through.

**The Song School:**
- Ground floor, south side of the close.
- Has CCTV covering the corridor outside.
- Locked from 7:30pm to 8am; opened only by master key.
- Marcus's office was on the first floor: wood-panelled, long horizontal window facing into the cloister, often unheated. Marcus used a 1962 Olivetti typewriter; refused email.
- Song School key serial numbers: RKF-001 (Rosalind Ferris / Precentor), RKF-002 (Marcus Vayne / Director of Music — now in police evidence), RKF-003 (Rose Hartley / Song School admin).
- 1998 Chapter resolution restricts Song School master key access to Precentor, Director of Music, and Song School administrative staff. No additional keys without majority Chapter resolution.
- Song School key ledger: spiral-bound paper ledger, in use since 1994, replaced three times without format change.

**The Lady Chapel:**
- Marcus's midday memorial service held here (Day 3, Ch 9).

**The Chapter House:**
- Edmund's office is the Chapter House annex, first floor.
- The archive is the cellar beneath the Chapter House.
- The Chapter House has a wall audible from Father Reeve's study/office — Marcus was heard through it two Wednesdays before his death.

**The Cloister:**
- Cloister garth (the enclosed garden). Jackdaws in cloister garth in May.
- Cloister east walk: Rosalind was observed here at approximately 12:30pm with a choir/music coverage schedule clipboard (Ch 13).

**The North Transept:**
- The organ loft stair is here.
- Thomas found Marcus at the foot of the north transept stair at approximately 6:50am.
- The north transept window Helen had been misidentifying is an Annunciation, not a martyrdom (Ch 13). LOCKED.

**Cathedral acoustics:**
- Helen's description: "vertical reverb — the building itself functioning as instrument." LOCKED.
- Amber-coloured when lit from below at the Wychford lower approach at night.

**Cathedral services (locked schedule):**
- Morning Prayer: 7am daily
- Eucharist: 8am Sunday, 10:30am Sunday
- Evensong: 5:30pm Monday–Saturday
- Chapter meetings: monthly, first Tuesday; chaired by the Dean; Edmund takes minutes. Emergency sessions possible by Dean's call (a special session was called on a Monday during the investigation, not the first Tuesday).

---

### WYCHFORD CITY

**Location:** Fictional English city. Somerset-adjacent. The county is not named. Do not name it in future books.
**Police station:** Red-brick 1970s building, Minster Lane at the inner ring road.
**Wychford Cathedral:** The cathedral's tower is lit from below at night, amber-coloured, visible from the lower approach.
**Key external locations:**
- King's Head pub: where Julian stayed (at least 3 nights post-murder) and drank his alibi pints.
- Wychford Pharmacy: Marcus's prescription source (digoxin, 0.125mg, label dated 28 January).

---

### THE CLOSE COMMUNITY — Roles and Rhythms

**Who lives in the close:**
- Helen Marsh (No. 7) — archive volunteer
- Edmund Hale (No. 2) — cathedral lawyer
- Canon Alistair Webb (No. 3) — canon, pastoral
- Canon Rosalind Ferris (No. 4, now vacant) — Precentor, convicted
- Very Revd Patricia Knowles — the Dean (deanery; precise numbering not established)
- Thomas Oakes — night verger (office behind south porch; residential location not established)
- Margaret Holt — Chapter clerk (residential location not established)
- Father Simon Reeve — chaplain (residential location not established)
- Rose Hartley — song school admin (residential location not established; may not live in the close)
- Two organ scholars (names not established in Book 1)
- A tenor (name not established in Book 1)
- Additional unnamed residents to total approximately 40

**Community rhythms:**
- The organ in rehearsal — muffled through the close walls, "always slightly eerie at a distance"
- The bell marking each hour — the close's heartbeat
- The cathedral acoustic pervades the entire close
- The close knew about Webb's release by Tuesday morning (information travels quickly inside a small community)
- Narcissi at No. 4 were watered (unknown party) by the Thursday after the arrest — still upright

---

### SENSORY ANCHORS (locked for all books)

These five sensory details define the world and must be present in every book:
1. **Cold stone and candle wax** — the cathedral smell, specific and inescapable
2. **The organ in rehearsal** — muffled through the close walls, always slightly eerie at a distance
3. **The damp chill of the archive cellar** — Helen's domain
4. **The particular quality of light through medieval stained glass** — coloured and specific, never the same twice; the east window's coloured light falling across the choir stalls in colours the thirteenth-century glaziers "didn't know they were choosing"
5. **The bell marking each hour** — the close's heartbeat

---

## SECTION 3 — LOCKED TIMELINE

### Book 1 — Death in the Cathedral Close

**Season:** April. Easter just passed. Cathedral slightly quieter than Lent.

**Year:** Present day. The exact year is not named and must not be named in any book.

**Investigation duration:** Approximately 50 days from the discovery of the body (Day 1) to Rosalind's arrest (approximately Day 50). The final chapter is May 14 (Tuesday).

**Key dates:**
| Event | When |
|-------|------|
| Rosalind begins embezzling from music fund | 12 years before Book 1 (approximately 2014) |
| Edmund writes financial policy requiring second signatory | 12 years before Book 1; stopped enforcing it 6 months later |
| Marcus loans Julian £12,000 | 9 years before Book 1 |
| Alistair Webb affair with Sarah Clifton begins | Approximately 3 years before Book 1 |
| Marcus discovers Rosalind's embezzlement | Approximately 3 months before Book 1 |
| Bridget Ferris dies, aged 87 | 18 months before Book 1 |
| Music fund withdrawals stop | Within one month of Bridget's death (approximately 17 months before Book 1) |
| Second digoxin bottle dispensed (Avon Private Dispensary, Clifton) | 11 March (approximately 5 weeks before the murder) |
| Marcus files complaint letter to Bishop | 14 March (approximately 4–5 weeks before the murder) |
| NOTE: CONTINUITY-REPORT.md Conflict M1 corrects original "3 weeks before story" to "approximately 4–5 weeks before story (14 March)" | Use 14 March / 4–5 weeks in all future books |
| Rosalind intercepts complaint letter | Approximately 3 weeks before the murder |
| Marcus demands Julian repay £17,000 | Approximately 6 weeks before the murder |
| Julian arrives in Wychford unexpectedly | Day before body found |
| Julian argues with Marcus at close gate | Afternoon before the murder night |
| Marcus visits Rosalind at No. 4 | Evening of the murder; leaves at approximately 9:30pm (Julian's testimony) |
| Rosalind arrives at organ loft with poisoned coffee | Approximately 10pm on the murder night |
| Marcus loses consciousness at organ | Approximately 10:15pm |
| Rosalind pushes Marcus from organ gallery | Approximately 10:30pm |
| Rosalind accesses archive storeroom via Song School CCTV corridor | 22:15:03–22:26:14 (11 minutes) |
| Thomas Oakes sees figure in pale blue cardigan leaving organ loft stair | 23:04 (4 past 11pm; his rounds were delayed by his niece's call) |
| Helen hears organ music and stopping | 2:02–approximately 2:10am |
| Thomas finds body | Approximately 6:50am (Day 1) |
| Helen examines scene | Day 1 morning |
| Post-mortem preliminary / murder confirmed | Day 3 |
| Webb arrested (false accusation) | Approximately Day 40, 7:40am Monday |
| Webb released | Approximately Day 41, 4:30pm Monday |
| Rosalind arrested | Approximately Day 50, 11:41am Thursday |
| Rosalind enters guilty plea | Before Chapter 38 (second week of May) |
| Helen finds 1887 letter | Wednesday, approximately Day 55+ (her regular Wednesday archive session) |
| Edmund reads Helen's photograph of the letter | 14 May (Tuesday), approximately 1pm — this is the final chapter date |
| Sentencing | Autumn of the year of the story |

**Murder night reconstructed sequence (per Ch 22 and Ch 34 — LOCKED):**
- ~9:30pm: Marcus leaves Rosalind's house (No. 4), walks steadily toward north transept direction. Witnessed by Julian Marsh.
- ~10pm: Rosalind arrives at organ loft with poisoned coffee. Marcus thanked her. It was cold.
- ~10:15pm: Marcus loses consciousness at the organ.
- ~10:30pm: Rosalind pushes his unconscious body from the organ gallery. "Marcus was not a small man." LOCKED.
- 22:15:03–22:26:14: Rosalind in archive storeroom (Song School CCTV), planting Marcus's original prescription bottle.
- 23:04: Thomas Oakes sees Rosalind leaving the organ loft stair in her pale blue cardigan (4 seconds, from 20 paces, from behind).

**NOTE on the original FACTS.md timeline entry:** The entry describing "Rosalind visits Marcus in organ room with poisoned coffee at ~9:30pm" is a misdescription. The correct sequence is Marcus visited Rosalind first, left her house at 9:30pm, and Rosalind followed him to the loft later. Future books must use the corrected sequence above.

---

## SECTION 4 — KILLER ARCHIVE
## SPOILER CONTENT — For series-continuity-guardian and series-manager only

### Book 1 — Death in the Cathedral Close

**Victim:** Marcus Vayne, 58, cathedral organist and director of music. Cause of death: digoxin toxicity. He lost consciousness at the organ and was pushed from the organ gallery (18 feet) by the killer. The fall obscured the cause of death until toxicology confirmed digoxin at a level several times his prescribed dose.

**Killer:** Canon Rosalind Ferris, 62, Precentor of Wychford Cathedral.

**Method:**
- Rosalind obtained a second bottle of digoxin through fraudulent means: Avon Private Dispensary, Clifton, Bristol. She presented as her deceased mother, Mrs. Bridget Ferris (using her mother's identity documents from the estate). The dispensing record: batch DA-6650/11, patient "Mrs. B. Ferris, Clifton Convalescent Home," 60 tablets digoxin 0.25mg (double Marcus's prescribed dose of 0.125mg), cash payment, dispensed 11 March.
- She drove to Bristol herself on 11 March to collect the tablets.
- She dissolved a lethal dose into Marcus's evening coffee. He had visited her at No. 4 that evening; she followed him to the organ loft at approximately 10pm, bringing the coffee. He thanked her. It was cold.
- He was unconscious by approximately 10:15pm.
- She carried him to the gallery rail and pushed him over. He was not a small man.
- The fall was intended to simulate an accident. It obscured cause of death until toxicology.
- She then went to the archive storeroom (via her Song School key) and planted Marcus's original prescription bottle (XR-0184/22, from Wychford Pharmacy) among the 1830s parish registers — to create a false trail suggesting he had left it there himself.
- She wiped the coffee mug clean, but a faint film of digoxin residue remained at the inner base.

**Motive:**
- Twelve years before Book 1, Rosalind began making small embezzling withdrawals from the cathedral music fund — individually trivial (£60–£110), cumulatively approximately £7,000/year — to cover the shortfall in her mother Bridget's dementia care home fees (Clifton Convalescent Home, Clifton, Bristol, approximately £4,000/month).
- Shell accounts used to launder the payments: J. Whitaker (Clifton, Bristol PO box, registered 12 years ago); L. Pellow Print Services (individual whose own address = another PO box); Aldgate Choral Supplies (no longer active). Approximately 47 off-pattern payments since 2014.
- Bridget Ferris died 18 months before Book 1, aged 87. Withdrawals stopped within a month.
- Approximately 3 months before Book 1, Marcus discovered the embezzlement. He gave Rosalind the opportunity to repay quietly. She presumably intended to.
- Marcus then withdrew the quiet exit and insisted on disclosure to the Chapter.
- She would have lost her position, pension, home, and 30 years of reputation.
- She made the decision over the course of one afternoon. Her first embezzlement withdrawal was £68. She had told herself she would replace it.
- NOTE: The decision to obtain the murder weapon (11 March dispensary visit) predates Marcus's formal complaint letter (14 March) by 3 days. The complaint made it urgent, not precipitating. She had already decided before he went to the Bishop.

**Opportunity window:**
- Rosalind had a Precentor's key to the cathedral and a Song School key (RKF-001, held 30 years).
- She had access to the archive storeroom via the Song School corridor.
- She had a pretext for visiting Marcus at the organ room — she sometimes checked on his rehearsals.

**How she was caught — the five genuine clues:**
1. **The coffee mug** (planted Ch 3, paid off Ch 10): The white china kitchen mug was on the second tread of the stair, oddly clean. Digoxin traces found in residue at the inner base — wiped, but not completely.
2. **The missing digoxin bottle** (planted Ch 4, paid off Ch 9 and Ch 28): Marcus's prescription bottle missing from his desk. Found in the archive storeroom behind 1830s parish registers — planted there by Rosalind. The batch number (XR-0184/22) matched Marcus's original prescription; the murder weapon was a separate bottle (DA-6650/11) obtained in Bristol.
3. **The false rehearsal lie** (planted Ch 5, paid off Ch 10): Rosalind told Helen she visited Marcus to remind him about Thursday's choir practice. Thursday's rehearsal had been cancelled three weeks earlier — in Rosalind's own marker-pen hand on the Music Term Plan binder. Edmund confirmed this via the binder and the spare grey ring-binder copy.
4. **The music fund** (planted Ch 6, paid off Ch 8, Ch 17, Ch 22): The music fund managed solely by Rosalind on a single signature — against financial policy Edmund himself wrote 12 years ago. Twelve years of small withdrawals to Bristol shell accounts, ultimately traced to Bridget Ferris's care home account. The £83 music fund payment on 8 March matched the estimated cost of 60 tablets of 0.25mg digoxin from a private dispensary (£60–£95).
5. **The pale blue cardigan** (planted Ch 3 setup, fully planted Ch 7, paid off Ch 28): Thomas Oakes saw a figure in a pale blue cashmere cardigan leaving the organ loft stair at 4 past 11pm. Song School CCTV placed a figure matching this description (5'6"–5'7", hip-length pale garment, hair up, asymmetry index 0.04) in the archive storeroom from 22:15:03 to 22:26:14. CCTV enhancement confirmed double-rib collar construction and cabled button band — specific cashmere garment consistent with Rosalind's known cardigan.

**How discovered (the reveal mechanism):**
- Helen identified the false rehearsal within 24 hours (Ch 5, confirmed Ch 10).
- Julian's testimony (Ch 11) contradicted Rosalind's stated departure time by 60+ minutes.
- Helen traced the music fund withdrawals to Bridget Ferris's care home account (Ch 17).
- The second digoxin bottle (Ch 15) and the dispensing record using Bridget Ferris's name (Ch 20) linked method to motive in the same fraudulent identity.
- Edmund identified that the £83 music fund withdrawal on 8 March matched the murder weapon's cost (Ch 22 — withheld from Helen and Claire until Ch 28).
- CCTV enhancement (Ch 28) confirmed the cardigan beyond reasonable doubt. All five clues paid off.
- Helen named Rosalind to DS Claire Marsh in Ch 19 (formally redirecting the investigation). The CCTV enhancement converted inference to documentary evidence.

**Key clues planted — fair-play guarantee:**
- All five clues are visible to a careful reader on re-read before the reveal in Chapters 32–35.
- The mug is explicitly named as oddly clean in Ch 3.
- The missing digoxin is named in Ch 4.
- The false rehearsal is in Rosalind's own dialogue in Ch 5.
- The music fund irregularity is named in Ch 6.
- The pale blue cardigan is described with precision in Ch 7.

**What the killer knew / didn't know:**
- Rosalind did not know Thomas Oakes was running 20–25 minutes late on his rounds the night of the murder (his niece had called). She expected him to have finished his rounds by 11pm; he came round at 4 past 11 and saw her leaving.
- Rosalind did not think about the Song School corridor CCTV.
- Rosalind knew that Edmund had been told about the music fund irregularity and was auditing it (she asked him about it directly, Ch 8). She assessed his progress in every conversation with him and Helen.
- Rosalind's question — "Whether Alistair is their only focus" — was her probe of the investigation's direction (Ch 23 and Ch 27). She asked it twice.
- She had Bridget's identity documents from the estate — this is how she obtained the fraudulent prescription.

**Status at end of Book 1:** Arrested. Guilty plea entered. Charge: murder. Embezzlement concurrent in separate proceedings. Sentencing: autumn of the year of the story. The pale blue cardigan is in police custody.

**The emotional truth (must be consistent in all future references):**
- Rosalind's confession (Ch 34): "I would have lost everything I have given my life to."
- Edmund to Rosalind: "Did you think about what Marcus gave his life to?" Rosalind: "Every day since." LOCKED.
- Edmund's formulation: "not betrayal. Comprehension without resolution." LOCKED.
- Helen's formulation: "She is a good person who did a terrible thing. That is not a contradiction. That is the hardest kind of case." LOCKED.
- The murder was an act of panic by a fundamentally decent person who made one small dishonest choice twelve years ago and couldn't stop. The reader feels this in the denouement.
- The embezzlement's human purpose (mother's care, dementia, private home fees) is part of the emotional texture. It does not excuse the murder. It explains the scale of what she feared losing.

---

## SECTION 5 — OPEN THREADS

These plot threads are unresolved at the end of Book 1. Book 2 must address or acknowledge them.

### Thread 1 — The 1887 Hartley Letter (PRIMARY SERIES THREAD)
**What it is:** A single folded sheet of heavy cream paper, dated July 1887, found by Helen in the archive (thirteenth file of the 1880s financial records box, placed slightly apart from the others, as if inserted later by a different hand). Salutation: "To the Hartley family, in settlement of the matter of the east transept." Payment acknowledged (amount not specified). Dean's signature. Final note: "The Chapter is grateful for the family's discretion and understanding."
**Where established:** Chapter 39 (Helen finds and photographs it). Chapter 40 (Edmund reads the photograph and makes the connection to Rose Hartley).
**Current in-world status:** Helen has set the 1880s financial records box aside with a note "Please do not re-file. To be looked at." Initialled by Helen. The letter is photographed; the photograph has been sent to Edmund (no message). Edmund's amber notebook entry, p.3: "Hartley family — July 1887 — east transept — settlement — cf. Helen's photograph, 14 May." + "Rose Hartley. Song school." + "I think there is something in the archive." Underlined once.
**What the reader knows:** A payment was made to the Hartley family in 1887. One of the Hartleys (Rose) works in the song school today. Something happened at the east transept.
**What the reader does NOT know (and must not know until Book 2):** Robert Hartley's name; Canon Aldous Pemberton's name; the specific nature of the 1887 wrong (stonemason's death, cover-up).
**Series hook delivered:** Edmund: "I think there's something in the archive." Helen: "I know. I've thought so for some time." LOCKED.
**Reader expectation:** Book 2 will pursue this thread.

### Thread 2 — Julian and Helen
**What it is:** The estrangement between Helen and her son Julian. Two years before Book 1, Julian chose to stop contact. By the end of Book 1, the estrangement door is no longer locked.
**Where established:** Chapters 4–5 (Julian arrives unexpectedly), 7 (they sit on the lych-gate bench), 11 (Julian's confession about the debt and his testimony about Marcus), 37 (the May kitchen conversation — the door opened).
**Current in-world status:** Julian returned the first weekend of May, acknowledged the pretext was transparent, and they talked in Helen's kitchen. He offered to pick up if she rang. She said "Yes. All right." He said "Take care of yourself, Mum." He has David's eyes and his grandmother's stubbornness. He lives in Bristol, works in data analytics.
**What remains open:** The debt to Marcus is now irrelevant (Marcus is dead). The estrangement's underlying causes — Julian's sense that Helen was always at work, his reading that she did the work "instead of" David — are acknowledged but not resolved. The relationship is unlocked, not repaired.
**Reader expectation:** Julian may appear in Book 2. His presence is not required but would be welcomed. He is not a red herring a second time — his innocence in Book 1 is fully established.
**Note:** Julian still has Helen's spare close gate key. This is a loose physical detail that can carry forward or be resolved simply.

### Thread 3 — Edmund's Feelings for Helen (Subtext Only)
**What it is:** The BLUEPRINT and SERIES-PLAN establish that Edmund has growing, unspoken feelings for Helen.
**Status in Book 1:** Entirely subtext. Never named. Never declared. Edmund sat in David Marsh's wing chair at No. 7 twice during the investigation (Ch 19 reference). He drove Helen back to the close after the station meeting. He is the only person whose interiority we share fully — and he does not name it.
**Series schedule:** SERIES-PLAN locks this as: declared in Book 2, accepted in Book 3. Do not advance it ahead of schedule. Do not name it in Book 2 narration until the moment of declaration.
**Reader expectation:** Present as warmth and partnership. The reader who is paying attention will feel it. It must not be stated.

### Thread 4 — Rose Hartley as Hartley Descendant
**What it is:** Rose Hartley works in the song school. She is a descendant of the Hartley family. She does not yet know the full significance of her family's connection to the cathedral.
**Status in Book 1:** Background character. Present throughout; described as descended from the Hartley family but not yet aware of her family's significance.
**What Book 2 must establish:** Rose becomes a central figure. She knows more than she initially admits. She has a grandmother's box — contents to be revealed in Book 3 (not Book 2). Book 2's closing hook from her: "There's one more thing. My grandmother left a box. I haven't opened it."

### Thread 5 — Webb's Affair and Its Aftermath
**What it is:** Canon Alistair Webb's affair with Sarah Clifton is now known to Edmund, and presumably to Claire and Anne. Webb was falsely arrested. His relationship with Anne is strained (she reached for his arm at his arrest and stopped herself).
**Status in Book 1:** Webb is cleared. He and Anne's relationship status is not resolved.
**What Book 2 must acknowledge:** Whether Webb is still in post. Whether Anne is still with him. Whether the affair is known beyond Edmund. This is a community consequence of the Book 1 investigation and should be referenced.

### Thread 6 — No. 4 Vacant
**What it is:** Rosalind's house at No. 4 is now vacant. The cathedral community has a physical reminder of her absence.
**Status in Book 1:** The narcissi in the terracotta pot on her windowsill were watered by an unknown party in the days after the arrest (still upright by Thursday after the arrest). No. 4 has an Oxford blue front door, a bay tree on the step, and a boot scraper worn smooth. Piano presumably still inside.
**Reader expectation:** In Book 2, the question of what happens to No. 4 — who moves in, whether it is left empty, whether the piano is removed — is a natural piece of close community texture.

### Thread 7 — Sentencing (Autumn)
**What it is:** Rosalind's sentencing for murder and embezzlement is scheduled for autumn of the year of the story. Edmund will provide a character statement.
**Status in Book 1:** Guilty plea entered. Sentencing pending.
**What Book 2 must acknowledge:** Whether the sentencing has occurred. If Book 2 is set in the following spring or summer, the sentencing may be referenced as having taken place. Edmund's character statement should be mentioned.

### Thread 8 — Edmund's Guilt and Resolution
**What it is:** Edmund had looked away from the music fund irregularity for 12 years. He had enforced the policy he wrote for six months and then let it lapse. He told the killer — three times, while she was auditing him — how close the investigation was.
**Status in Book 1:** Resolved internally, not publicly. His May 1 notebook entry: "I chose truth. The institution survived it. I had not expected that." His formulation: "He had confused looking away with discretion. He will not make this confusion again." LOCKED.
**Reader expectation:** This resolution shapes Edmund's character going into Book 2. He is now a man who has tested his institutional loyalty and found it wanting — and found the institution survived the test. This is his arc completion. Book 2 should not re-run this arc; it can assume Edmund's new position.

### Thread 9 — The 1880 Close Map
**What it is:** Helen found an uncatalogued 1880 close map showing the song school in a slightly different position (slightly south, smaller). The Precentor's house has always been No. 4.
**Status in Book 1:** Found, noted, not further explored. May be relevant to the east transept investigation in Book 2 — the song school's original position relative to the east transept may matter.

---

## SECTION 6 — READER PROMISES

What this series has promised its readers that every future book must deliver.

**The fair-play contract:**
Every clue must be visible to the careful reader before the reveal. No information may be withheld from the narrator. The killer must appear throughout the book as a fully-drawn, likeable character. The denouement must account for every clue and every red herring retrospectively.
- Book 1 used five genuine clues and four red herrings. This sets a benchmark. Future books should target a similar density of fair-play evidence.
- The reveal mechanism should include: an Assembly beat (Helen walks through every clue in order), a Killer's Truth beat (the killer speaks honestly), and a Cost beat (what the investigation took from the community). This three-beat denouement structure is the series convention.

**The Close community:**
Cases happen within the world of Wychford Cathedral Close. The killer is always someone Helen and Edmund know and care about. The close has a finite population — approximately 40 permanent residents — and the reader accumulates knowledge of this community across books. Characters who survive one book are present or accounted for in the next.

**The tone:**
Warm darkness — not cosy fluff, not grim thriller. Literary but accessible. The BLUEPRINT logline: "A book you cannot put down after chapter three, that makes you love two people by chapter eight, that makes you suspect everyone by chapter twenty, and that makes you feel something complicated in the final pages — not just satisfaction, but grief for what the cathedral close lost when the truth came out."
- The French technique: wrongness felt before wrongness named. This is the series opening rhythm — every book must open with dread before the murder is named.
- The Osman warmth with Christie plotting: the warmth is not cutesy. It is earned. Edmund's dry wit, Helen's understatement, Margaret's institutional comedy — these are not decoration. They are the load-bearing warmth that makes the darkness land.

**The protagonists:**
Helen and Edmund work together. Neither solves it alone. Maximum two primary POVs — Helen and Edmund only. No other character's interiority, ever. Helen on odd chapters, Edmund on even chapters (shifts permitted for structural reason with explicit justification in handoff).

**The cathedral itself:**
The cathedral is a character. It absorbs seven centuries of human behaviour without comment. It continues. Whatever the close loses — a Precentor, a trust, an illusion — Morning Prayer happens at 7am, Evensong at 5:30pm, the organ plays, the coloured light falls through the east window. This is not indifference; it is the cathedral's form of permanence. Every book must end with this note.
- Final image of Book 1 (locked): "coloured light from east window, the organ playing, the close and cathedral continuing. It was not finished with them yet."
- Every book's final image should echo this.

**The series theme:**
"The things institutions protect are not always the things worth protecting." Each book must carry this theme — applied to a new contemporary murder and one more layer of the 1887 historical wrong.

**The historical thread:**
One more fragment of the 1887 secret per book. The reader accumulates understanding. Every book ends with one door opened in the historical thread. The fragment must be enough to satisfy without being enough to resolve. The resolution is reserved for Book 3.

**Specific promises made in Book 1 text that must not be broken:**
- Edmund will not again confuse looking away with discretion. Do not give him a scene in Book 2 where he reverts to institutional cover-up.
- The estrangement door is no longer locked. Do not re-lock it in Book 2. Julian and Helen's relationship can be strained, distant, or complicated, but the door must not re-close entirely.
- The guilty plea is entered. Rosalind is not going to be exonerated. Do not introduce a Book 2 plot that undermines the Book 1 verdict.
- Edmund will provide a character statement at Rosalind's sentencing. This must be mentioned or acknowledged in Book 2 if the sentencing falls within Book 2's timeline.
- The series hook ("I think there's something in the archive" / "I know. I've thought so for some time.") is a direct reader promise. Book 2 must pursue the archive thread. It cannot be deferred again.

---

## SECTION 7 — VOICE BIBLE

### Helen Marsh — Speech and Thought Patterns

**Core register:** Precise, wry, occasionally devastating. She does not perform warmth — she earns it.

**Sentence structure:** Short declarative sentences. She does not hedge. She does not qualify unless she is genuinely uncertain, in which case she says "I note" not "I think."

**What she says:**
- "I note" rather than "I think"
- "He didn't fall, Edmund. Look at his hands." — statement of fact delivered as invitation
- "I wonder if you might come to No. 7 this afternoon" — formal, precise, slightly ironic
- "Give me an hour." is what Claire says; Helen's equivalent is the silence that accepts it
- "Yes. All right." to Julian — two words, enormous emotional content
- "I always do." in response to "take care of yourself" — and her acknowledgment that he's revising his assessment

**What she never says:**
- "I feel" (she narrates feelings as physical observations: "her hands trembled briefly")
- "I think" (it's always "I note")
- She never volunteers emotion. She never performs grief or shock. When she breaks composure it is physical: hands trembling briefly at the sight of the digoxin bottle.

**Her internal voice:**
- Her notebook entry discipline: single words, not sentences. "mug."
- Her formulations are terse and final: "motive before method is the correct order."
- She registers everything. She names it later, if at all. The gap between registration and naming is where her character lives.
- On Rosalind: "She is a good person who did a terrible thing. That is not a contradiction. That is the hardest kind of case." This is her moral register — not simple, not sentimental, not cynical.
- She does not knock at Edmund's door when she wants to but doesn't. She stops and does not knock. (Ch 35 — she stopped at Edmund's door and did not knock.)

**Her humour:**
- Understatement. "Oh God" from Claire is the warmth she provokes in others by being herself. Helen does not joke. She observes with precision and occasionally the precision is funny.
- She received "You're good at this, Mum" without dismantling it. "I know." This is not arrogance; it is the beginning of receiving.

**Her characteristic emotional state at end of Book 1:**
- Beginning to let someone in — the arc movement completed.
- "The complicated warmth of something that is not resolution but is progress; a door that is no longer locked."

---

### Edmund Hale — Speech and Thought Patterns

**Core register:** Careful, measured, with sudden flashes of dry wit. Long sentences that double back on themselves. Genuine faith that is quiet and not performed.

**Sentence structure:** His sentences qualify, return, and consider. He uses subordinate clauses to hold complexity. He does not rush to conclusion.

**What he says:**
- "I wonder if..." when he means "I know."
- Occasional Latin — always translated immediately, always wryly. *Stultissimus* — used privately without translation (a rare exception, indicating self-directed severity).
- "She was very good at this." (watching the killer chair a meeting) — the understatement is its own form of knowing.
- "I know." (said twice to Webb in the interview room, on record — signalling to the reader that the arrest is wrong)
- "She had thirty years of say in it. The momentum doesn't stop the week she leaves."
- "He was, in his way, a better person than the circumstance allowed him to be."
- "I think there's something in the archive." — his closing line of the book. Precise, quiet, enormous.

**His notebook as voice:**
- Underlining "wonder" twice = "I already know" (his private code)
- Underlining other words once = significance noted, not resolved
- Writing initials rather than full names = the moment suspicion becomes near-certainty ("RF")
- Circling empty space = absence of evidence that is itself evidence
- His last investigation entry was three words about the arrest warrant, underlined once.
- The brown battered notebook is now closed and will not be opened "in the close in quite the same way again." LOCKED.
- The amber notebook: pages 1–3 used, 17th notebook from the cathedral precinct shop.

**His formality level:**
- High but not stiff. He addresses people correctly by title and name. He modulates toward warmth with Helen and Margaret.
- He is capable of saying "I know" in a police interview room — an act of small courage in institutional language.

**His theological reference:**
- Quiet faith. He does not cite scripture for effect. Faith emerges in his composure, his patience, and his willingness (at Book 1's end) to choose truth over institution.
- *Resolutio* — the loosening of what was bound — is how he experiences resolution. He would use this Latin without explaining it to himself; he would probably not say it aloud.

**His emotional restraint:**
- He felt "something close to shame" watching Webb leave the police station. This is as close to direct emotional statement as he gets. It is locked.
- He closed his notebook in the interview room with Rosalind without remembering he had opened it. LOCKED.
- He hoped the waiting had not been entirely terrible (re: the arrest). LOCKED. This is hope delivered as institutional understatement.

**His dry wit:**
- Emerges under stress. Not jokey. It is the form his humanity takes when he cannot be openly human.
- "Fourteen minutes from the end of the meeting. I went to my office first." (to Helen's "I wondered how long.") — precise, self-aware, warm.

---

### Prose Style Anchors

**Sentence rhythm:** The book alternates between Helen's short declarative rhythm and Edmund's longer, qualifying rhythm. This is the series' fundamental engine. Do not make Helen's chapters long and meditative, and do not make Edmund's chapters clipped and efficient. The POV voice shapes the prose.

**Description style:**
- Physical observations first, emotional readings second (and often not stated at all — left for the reader).
- Sensory precision: the particular quality of coloured light, the specific weight of a cardigan, the exact time on CCTV footage. Precision is the series' aesthetic.
- Atmosphere through specificity, not adjective accumulation. "Cold stone and candle wax" not "the ancient, mysterious, atmospheric cathedral."

**How the narrator handles time:**
- The investigation timeline is precise — chapters are dated by day number or specific date. This precision is load-bearing for the mystery (the 14 March complaint date, the 11 March dispensary visit, the 22:15:03 CCTV timestamp).
- In the final chapters (35–40), time loosens — we move through days and weeks without precise timestamps. This is intentional: the investigation is over, and the close is returning to its own slower rhythms.

**POV conventions:**
- Strict dual POV: Helen on odd chapters, Edmund on even chapters. No other character's interiority.
- POV shifts mid-book are permitted but must be explicitly justified in the handoff.
- The interiority is close third — deeply internal, but described in a register consistent with the character's voice. Helen's interiority sounds like Helen. Edmund's sounds like Edmund.
- No omniscient drift. The narrator knows only what the POV character can know.

**The close and the cathedral as recurring presence:**
- The cathedral continues regardless. Every book must contain at least one moment where the close's rhythms (bells, organ, services, morning light) are noted as continuing despite the human disruption.
- Jackdaws in the cloister garth in May — a specific, accurate, English detail. The kind of small observed fact that grounds the world.

---

### What to Avoid

**Too fluffy / too cosy:**
- Do not undercut the darkness with lightness that isn't earned. Margaret Holt's comedy is earned by her 40-year tenure and her genuine love of the institution. It is not decoration. If a comic moment doesn't arise naturally from character, cut it.
- Do not let Helen or Edmund be warm in a performed way. Warmth in this series is what emerges despite restraint, not because of it.
- Do not soften the emotional cost of the final reveal. The reader should feel grief, not just satisfaction.

**Too graphic / too thriller:**
- The murder method (digoxin poisoning, fall from a height) is clinical, planned, and almost bloodless. Future murders should match this register. No torture, no elaborate gore, no thriller-style action sequences.
- The investigation is conducted through observation, conversation, document-reading, and inference — not chases, confrontations, or physical danger to the protagonists.
- Helen and Edmund are 70 and 68. They are not action heroes. Their power is forensic and legal intelligence.

**Too procedural:**
- The police process is present but not foregrounded. DS Claire Marsh (DI by Book 2) is a contact and occasional ally, not the protagonist. The investigation is Helen and Edmund's, conducted in parallel with but not subordinate to the police inquiry.
- Do not introduce forensic science jargon for its own sake. Helen's forensic expertise is rendered in plain language and physical observation. "He didn't fall. Look at his hands." Not a forensic report.

**Too academic:**
- Edmund's ecclesiastical law and institutional knowledge are present as flavour and practical intelligence, not as extended exposition. He knows the Cathedrals Measure; he does not recite it.
- Historical research (the 1887 thread) should surface through physical objects and found documents — letters, maps, ledgers — not through lectures or research montages.

**Continuity traps to watch for in Book 2:**
- Rosalind is gone. Do not bring her back in any form that undermines the guilty plea. She may be referenced (sentencing, Edmund's character statement, No. 4's vacancy, the narcissi) but should not appear.
- Robert Hartley must NOT have been named in Book 1. Do not write Book 2 scenes that imply he was.
- Canon Aldous Pemberton's portrait is in the Chapter House — it was mentioned but not significant in Book 1. It may become significant in Book 2 without contradiction.
- Julian's alibi (corrected): King's Head pub approximately 7pm, departed around 9:30pm, two pints. Do not revert to the earlier "9pm to last orders" version.
- Rosalind's tenure: 30 years (she preceded Marcus by approximately six years). Do not use "came year after Marcus."
- The dispensary visit was approximately 5 weeks before the murder (11 March to 14 April = ~34 days), not 6 weeks.
- The murder night sequence: Marcus left Rosalind's house at 9:30pm, then Rosalind followed him to the organ loft. Not "Rosalind visited Marcus at 9:30pm."
